sanskrit_iast:
krodhād bhavati sammohaḥ sammohāt smṛti-vibhramaḥ |
smṛti-bhraṁśād buddhi-nāśo buddhi-nāśāt praṇaśyati || 63 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape, body text byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; uses single danda | as terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.63।।; spelling 'संमोहः' (anusvara) where vedabase prints 'सम्मोहः' (geminate consonants); same Sanskrit conjunct)
IAST exact (Devanagari rendered without transliteration marks; IAST body matches: 'krodhad bhavati sammohah sammohat smrtivibhramah smrtibhramsad buddhinaso buddhinasat pranasyati')

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.063 = MBh_06,024.063). Body text identical: 'krodhād bhavati saṃmohaḥ saṃmohāt smṛtivibhramaḥ / smṛtibhraṃśād buddhināśo buddhināśāt praṇaśyati'. GRETIL writes 'saṃmohaḥ' / 'saṃmohāt' / 'smṛtibhraṃśād' with anusvara where vedabase prints 'sammohaḥ' / 'sammohāt' / 'smṛti-bhraṁśād' (geminate / candrabindu); same Sanskrit conjuncts.

anvaya.meaning: delusion / complete illusion / loss of discrimination
confusion / bewilderment (in compound smṛti-vibhramaḥ: 'confusion of memory')
from loss / from collapse (in compound smṛti-bhraṁśād: 'from loss of memory')
destruction / loss (in compound buddhi-nāśaḥ: 'destruction of intelligence')
from destruction (in compound buddhi-nāśāt: 'from destruction of intelligence')
is utterly destroyed / perishes / falls down

translations.verbatim_quote: From anger, complete delusion arises, and from delusion bewilderment of memory. When memory is bewildered, intelligence is lost, and when intelligence is lost one falls down again into the material pool.
Anger leads to clouding of judgment, which results in bewilderment of memory. When memory is bewildered, the intellect gets destroyed; and when the intellect is destroyed, one is ruined.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Śrīla Rūpa Gosvāmī has given us this direction: One should not desire — not even for those things which are useful for spiritual realization — if such desire is not authorized by śāstra. The development of such desires leads gradually to delusion, and so to bewilderment of memory of one's actual position, the loss of which means destruction of intelligence and finally the loss of the goal of human life altogether.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (Shankara comments on 2.62-2.63 together as one chained argument; this excerpt covers the 2.63 portion of that chain)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Krodhat, from anger; bhavati, follows; sammohah, delusion, absence of discrimination with regard to what should or should not be done. Indeed, an angry person, becoming deluded, abuses even his preceptor. Sammohat, from delusion; smṛti-vibhramaḥ, the loss of memory which arises from the impressions [acquired earlier] from the scriptures and the teacher. After this comes buddhi-nāśaḥ, loss of intelligence; for the mind which has lost the memory of what is conducive cannot discriminate. From the loss of intelligence, praṇaśyati — he is destroyed, he becomes incapable of his human goal.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads 2.63 as the second half of the cascade begun in 2.62 and treats them as one chained argument. From krodha (anger) arises sammoha (delusion — specifically the loss of discrimination between what should and should not be done; an angry person abuses even his teacher). From sammoha arises smṛti-vibhrama (loss of memory of what was learned from scripture and teacher; the saṁskāras of right learning are obliterated). From smṛti-vibhrama arises buddhi-nāśa (loss of the discriminating faculty; the mind that has lost its inherited memory cannot discriminate). From buddhi-nāśa arises praṇaśyati — utter destruction; the loss of the human goal itself. Each step is a distinct cognitive failure, and the cascade is structurally one-directional: each step requires its precondition, and the final step is the loss of the operator as a functioning rational agent.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: From the loss of memory there comes the destruction of discrimination. The meaning is that there will be destruction of the effect of efforts made earlier to attain the knowledge of the self. From the destruction of discrimination, one becomes lost, i.e., is sunk in Samsara or worldliness.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja's reading on 2.63 emphasises the destruction-of-prior-effort dimension. From smṛti-bhraṁśa (loss of memory) comes buddhi-nāśa, which Ramanuja explicitly glosses as 'the destruction of the effect of efforts made earlier to attain knowledge of the self'. The cascade does not merely degrade present judgment — it undoes the operator's accumulated work toward self-knowledge. From buddhi-nāśa, the operator becomes 'lost' — sunk in saṁsāra. The structural claim: the cascade obliterates not only the current decision but the entire trajectory of practice that brought the operator to the threshold of decision.

positions.rendering: delusion — absence of discrimination with regard to what should or should not be done
delusion as the cognitive obscuration that precedes memory-loss

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: sammoha (literally 'thorough deluding-confusion') is a strong term. Shankara's gloss is the most operationally precise: it is specifically the loss of discrimination about what should and should not be done — an angry person abuses even the teacher. Modern translators render variously. Mukundananda's 'clouding of judgment' is the softest rendering and risks understatement; Prabhupada's 'complete delusion' preserves the strength but loses the operational specificity. The verse's chain depends on sammoha being strong enough to obliterate the next step (smṛti — memory of right learning); a mere clouding cannot do that. Shankara's reading is therefore the lexically tightest.

positions.rendering: one falls down again into the material pool
he is destroyed, he becomes incapable of his human goal
becomes lost, i.e., is sunk in Samsara or worldliness

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: praṇaśyati is the cascade's terminal verb. The four readings differ in how cosmologically loaded they make it. Prabhupada and Ramanuja import the saṁsāra frame explicitly; Shankara locates it at the level of the human goal (puruṣārtha) without the rebirth machinery; Mukundananda uses the most generic 'ruined'. The structural claim across all four is that the cascade ends in the destruction of the operator as a functioning rational agent — not metaphorical, not graded, but full loss of the conditions that made the operator's prior practice intelligible. Shankara's gloss ('incapable of his human goal') is the most precise without importing tradition-specific cosmology.

literal_meaning: From anger, delusion arises; from delusion, confusion of memory; from collapse of memory, destruction of intelligence; from destruction of intelligence, one perishes.
traditional_meaning_consensus: This verse completes the eight-step psychological cascade begun in 2.62. Steps 5-8: (5) sammoha — delusion, specifically the loss of discrimination between what should and should not be done; the angry operator now offends even those whose authority she would have respected an hour earlier. (6) smṛti-vibhrama — confusion or collapse of memory, specifically of what was learned from teacher and scripture; the saṁskāras of right learning are obliterated. (7) buddhi-nāśa — destruction of the discriminating faculty itself; the operator can no longer reason because the substrate of reasoning (the inherited learning that gives reasoning its grounding) has been destroyed. (8) praṇaśyati — utter destruction; loss of the human goal, the operator's end-purpose itself. Shankara reads 2.62-2.63 as one chained argument. Ramanuja emphasises that buddhi-nāśa undoes the operator's prior effort toward self-knowledge — the cascade obliterates not only the current decision but the entire practice trajectory. All traditions agree the cascade is sequential, one-directional, and terminal in its full extent. Together with 2.62 it forms one of the Gita's most precise psychological analyses, and the prevention is upstream — at the dhyāna step in 2.62 — not at any step in 2.63.

remaining_gaps: Shankara's full Sanskrit bhashya not pulled — only translator-rendered (Gambhirananda) excerpt was machine-extractable; Shankara historically combines 2.62-2.63 as one chained argument.
